label: Confirmed Dead (Official)
contestNote: Official figure of 30 is widely rejected. Estimates from journalists, survivors, and researchers range from 200 to 400 dead. The 1993 National Security Archive documents cite at least 200–300 killed.

label: Estimated Dead (Researchers)
contestNote: Journalist Elena Poniatowska's landmark 1971 book "La Noche de Tlatelolco" and declassified NSA cables from 1968 suggest death tolls far exceeding official figures. Some witnesses report bodies removed by army trucks overnight.

label: Detained After Massacre
contestNote: Estimates of those detained range from 1,500 to 2,500. Many were held at Campo Militar No. 1 (Military Camp 1) without charge. Exact numbers remain unknown.

label: Successful Prosecutions
contestNote: Despite the 2006 FEMOSPP report naming Díaz Ordaz, Echeverría, and senior military officers as responsible for genocide and crimes against humanity, all charges were ultimately dropped or dismissed. No perpetrator has served prison time.

label: Declassified US Documents
source: NSA / CIA / State Dept via National Security Archive
contestNote: Hundreds of US government documents released since 1997 through Freedom of Information Act requests by the National Security Archive reveal CIA and NSA surveillance of Mexican student movement and knowledge of the massacre.

label: Student Strike Duration (1968)
contestNote: The CNH (Consejo Nacional de Huelga) strike ran from approximately July 22 to October 2, 1968 — ended by the massacre. It involved UNAM, IPN, and dozens of other Mexican institutions.

label: Days to Olympics at Time of Massacre
contestNote: The 1968 Mexico City Summer Olympics opened October 12, 1968 — just 10 days after the massacre. The government's desire to project stability before the world stage is considered a key factor in the decision to use lethal force.
